evals.prompt: We're evaluating whether to adopt Apache Kafka for our event streaming needs. We currently use RabbitMQ but are hitting scaling limits at about 50k messages/sec. Can you do a full technology assessment of Kafka for our use case?
evals.expected_output: A structured technology assessment of Apache Kafka covering TRL, hype cycle position, evaluation matrix with scored dimensions, risk assessment, and actionable recommendations

deterministic_checks.description: Assessment includes Technology Readiness Level classification
Assessment maps Kafka to a Gartner Hype Cycle position
Assessment includes a trend indicator for Kafka
Assessment includes scored evaluation dimensions (1-5 scale)
Assessment includes risk analysis
Assessment names the technology being assessed
Assessment references user's current context
Assessment includes recommendations section
Assessment includes monitoring indicators per output template

expectations: Kafka is assessed at TRL 7-9 (production/mature) given its widespread enterprise adoption
The evaluation matrix scores Technical Maturity, Strategic Fit, Risk Profile, and Economic Factors each on a 1-5 scale
Recommendations are actionable and specific, not vague platitudes

evals.prompt: We need to decide whether to build our own authentication system or buy an identity platform like Auth0 or Okta. We're a 30-person B2B SaaS startup with about 500 enterprise customers. Can you run the build vs buy framework on this?
evals.expected_output: A structured build vs buy analysis using the weighted scoring matrix from the skill, with a clear recommendation and rationale

deterministic_checks.description: Assessment explicitly frames the build vs buy comparison
Assessment uses the weighted scoring factors from the Build vs Buy Matrix
Assessment includes factor weights from the framework
Assessment produces a weighted total score and recommendation
Assessment evaluates specific buy options mentioned by user
Assessment applies Build When/Buy When criteria from the framework
Assessment references the user's team size or customer base

expectations: The analysis uses the 5-factor weighted matrix (Strategic importance 30%, Differentiation 25%, Time to market 20%, Total cost 15%, Risk 10%)
Both Build and Buy options receive numeric scores for each factor
The recommendation leans toward Buy given that auth is commodity functionality for a B2B SaaS, matching the 'Buy When' criteria in the skill

evals.prompt: Do a tech stack analysis for our platform. We're running on AWS with ECS for containers, PostgreSQL on RDS, Redis for caching, a React frontend with Next.js, and a Go backend. We also use Terraform for IaC and GitHub Actions for CI/CD. I want to know where our stack is strong and where we have gaps.
evals.expected_output: A layered tech stack analysis covering Infrastructure, Platform, and Application layers with evaluation criteria scores and gap identification

deterministic_checks.description: Assessment covers the three stack layers from the skill framework
Assessment evaluates AWS as the cloud provider
Assessment evaluates ECS as the compute layer
Assessment uses Stack Evaluation Criteria from the skill
Assessment identifies gaps or areas for improvement
Assessment evaluates PostgreSQL from user's stack
Assessment evaluates Redis from user's stack
Assessment evaluates Next.js from user's stack
Assessment evaluates Terraform from user's stack

expectations: Technologies are organized into the three layers (Infrastructure, Platform, Application) from the skill framework
Each technology is evaluated against the Stack Evaluation Criteria (Performance, Scalability, Maintainability, Security, Cost, Talent, Community)
Trend indicators (INC/DEC/CONST) are applied to at least some of the technologies in the stack

evals.prompt: We're looking at acquiring a small AI startup. Can you give me a technical due diligence checklist and assessment framework to evaluate their technology?
evals.expected_output: A comprehensive technical due diligence framework covering Architecture Review, Code Quality, Operations, and Team assessment areas

deterministic_checks.description: Assessment includes Architecture Review section from the due diligence framework
Assessment includes Code Quality section
Assessment includes Operations section
Assessment includes Team evaluation with key person dependency or retention considerations
Assessment addresses concrete evaluation criteria not just section headers
Operations section covers deployment or monitoring specifics
Architecture review covers scalability or design patterns
Assessment is contextualized to AI/ML startup specifics

expectations: All four due diligence areas are covered: Architecture Review, Code Quality, Operations, Team
Each area includes the specific sub-items from the skill (e.g., Architecture Review covers system design, scalability, technical debt, documentation quality)
The framework is tailored to AI startup specifics (model quality, training data, ML pipeline) not just generic software due diligence
Key person dependency and retention risk are explicitly called out given it's a small startup

evals.prompt: assess webassembly as a technology for us - we're thinking about using it for running user-submitted code in a sandbox on our platform
evals.expected_output: A technology assessment of WebAssembly with TRL classification appropriate for an emerging-but-maturing technology, hype cycle position, and evaluation against the sandboxed code execution use case

deterministic_checks.description: Assessment includes TRL classification
Assessment includes Hype Cycle positioning
Assessment addresses the sandboxing use case specifically
Assessment includes numeric scores in the evaluation matrix
Assessment names the technology being assessed
Assessment includes recommendations
Assessment includes risk mitigation per output template

expectations: WebAssembly is assessed at TRL 6-8 range reflecting its transition from development to production readiness
Security and isolation capabilities are evaluated in depth given the sandboxed code execution use case
The assessment mentions specific WebAssembly runtimes (Wasmtime, Wasmer, V8) as implementation options
Risks specific to user-submitted code execution are identified (escape vulnerabilities, resource limits, API surface)

evals.prompt: compare kubernetes vs nomad vs ecs for our container orchestration. we have 3 devops engineers and run about 40 microservices
evals.expected_output: A competitive technology analysis comparing three container orchestration platforms using the skill's comparative framework with trend indicators

deterministic_checks.description: Assessment evaluates Kubernetes
Assessment evaluates Nomad
Assessment evaluates ECS
Assessment uses trend indicators from the skill framework
Assessment identifies strengths and weaknesses for each option
Assessment references the team size constraint
Assessment references the 40 microservices scale factor
Assessment makes a clear recommendation

expectations: All three technologies are assessed side-by-side using the Competitive Technology Analysis table format from the skill
Each technology gets a trend indicator (INC/DEC/CONST) with justification
The assessment factors in team size (3 DevOps engineers) when evaluating operational complexity
A clear recommendation is made with rationale tied to the user's specific constraints, not just a neutral comparison

evals.prompt: technology assessment for blockchain in our supply chain tracking product
evals.expected_output: A technology assessment of blockchain for supply chain that honestly addresses the hype cycle position and evaluates whether it fits the specific use case

deterministic_checks.description: Assessment includes TRL classification
Assessment maps blockchain to the Hype Cycle
Assessment is contextualized to supply chain use case
Assessment identifies risks or challenges
Assessment names the technology
Assessment considers alternatives to blockchain
Assessment provides recommendations

expectations: Blockchain for supply chain is positioned appropriately on the Hype Cycle (Trough of Disillusionment or Slope of Enlightenment depending on the specific application)
The evaluation matrix scores are realistic and not inflated - blockchain has known economic and scalability challenges for supply chain
The assessment considers alternatives (e.g., centralized databases with audit trails) as part of the competitive position analysis
Build vs Buy considerations are addressed given that supply chain blockchain platforms exist (IBM Food Trust, VeChain)
